name: Ace of Diamonds Mine
short_description: Historic Herkimer County diamond site featuring double-terminated quartz crystals (Herkimer Diamonds) and fluorite in a low-elevation, accessible setting.
description:
Ace of Diamonds Mine is a notable rockhounding destination in Herkimer County, New York, the birthplace of the famous Herkimer Diamond. Located in the Town of German Flatts at 546 feet elevation on gentle slopes, this site offers collectors the chance to find quartz crystals, fluorite, and calcite specimens characteristic of the region's Ordovician dolostone formations.

The Herkimer County area sits within the Mohawk Valley, where Paleozoic sedimentary rocks were deposited in an ancient ocean environment roughly 450 million years ago. As these rocks were buried and exposed to hydrothermal fluids over geological time, cavities within the dolomite formed and filled with quartz solutions that crystallized into the distinctive double-terminated, transparent quartz crystals known as Herkimer Diamonds. Fluorite precipitation from the same mineral-rich fluids created the purple and green fluorite specimens often found alongside the quartz.

This site is relatively easy to work with accessible terrain and a long history of amateur collecting. The gentle slope and moderate elevation make it suitable for family outings and beginning rockhounds. Specimens range from small crystals to larger display pieces, and the combination of quartz, fluorite, and calcite makes each visit potentially rewarding.

Visitors should expect a rural, low-traffic environment typical of upstate New York's farming and mining heritage. The site reflects decades of interest from local and regional collectors, and responsible collecting practices ensure the location remains productive for future rockhounds.
history: The Ace of Diamonds Mine exploits the same Ordovician dolostone formation that produces Herkimer Diamonds throughout the region. During the Paleozoic Era, marine sedimentation deposited these limestone and dolomite layers, which later underwent diagenesis and hydrothermal alteration that filled small cavities with silica-rich solutions, crystallizing the characteristic double-terminated quartz crystals over millions of years.
tips: Search along the base of exposed dolostone outcrops and in loose rubble piles where weathering has released crystals from the host rock. Use a small chisel or flathead screwdriver to carefully extract crystals from the dolomite without breaking them - work gently along natural fracture lines. Look for the characteristic clear, glassy, double-pointed quartz crystals ranging from 0.5 to 2 inches; purple or green fluorite crystals are often found in the same pockets. Bring a headlamp or flashlight to peer into small cavities and crevices where the best crystals are often hidden.
terrain_notes: Elevation 546 feet with gentle slopes characteristic of the Mohawk Valley region. Terrain consists of exposed and weathered Ordovician dolostone with scattered loose rock and small rubble piles. The site is walkable in sturdy footwear; no extreme elevation changes or hazardous scrambling required. Access involves short walks from parking areas to collecting zones, typically under half a mile of easy walking.
best_season: May - September. Late spring through early fall offers the most pleasant weather for outdoor rockhounding in upstate New York. Summers provide warm, dry conditions ideal for fieldwork. Late fall and winter are possible but involve mud, snow, and shorter daylight hours that reduce productivity.
written_directions: From the Town of German Flatts village center, head north on NY Route 5S toward the Mohawk Valley area. Ace of Diamonds Mine is located approximately 2 miles north of the town center near known dolostone quarry areas. Look for small dirt pullouts and informal parking areas along rural roads approaching the collecting site. GPS coordinates 43.011, -74.972 will direct you to the general vicinity; local knowledge or inquiry with regional rockhounding clubs may be necessary for precise site access points, as the exact mine entrance may be on private land or require landowner permission.
road_conditions: Rural roads in varying condition. Expect unpaved or partially paved rural roadways typical of upstate New York farming country. High-clearance vehicles may be beneficial for some approach roads, but passenger cars can generally access the general vicinity.
parking_notes: Informal parking areas; likely small dirt pullouts near the site. Capacity is very limited and parking may depend on current site conditions and landowner permission. Always ask permission before parking on or near private property.
collection_rules: This is believed to be private land. Permission from the landowner is essential before collecting. Respect all posted signs and boundaries. No commercial collection. Take only what you can use personally. Do not remove large rocks or damage the site with power tools or explosives.
rules:
Obtain landowner permission before accessing and collecting
No commercial collection or sales of specimens
Personal use only
Respect all posted property boundaries
Fill any holes dug and leave the site as you found it
No motorized equipment without explicit permission
nearest_services: Herkimer village approximately 3-4 miles south offers gas, food, water, and supplies. The town of German Flatts has limited services; the larger Herkimer and Mohawk area nearby provides full amenities.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain on gentle slopes accessible to most hikers with basic fitness. Sturdy footwear recommended due to loose rock and sharp crystal fragments underfoot. No wheelchair access to active collecting areas, but the overall terrain is not steep or hazardous. Short walks from parking area to collecting zones make this suitable for families and those with moderate mobility.

faq.question: Can I find Herkimer Diamonds at Ace of Diamonds Mine in New York?
faq.answer: Yes, Ace of Diamonds Mine is located in Herkimer County and targets the same Ordovician dolostone formation that produces the region's famous Herkimer Diamonds. You can find double-terminated quartz crystals, often called Herkimer Diamonds, along with fluorite and calcite at this site.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Ace of Diamonds Mine free or do I need to pay a fee?
faq.answer: Ace of Diamonds Mine is private land, so there is no official fee structure. However, you must obtain permission from the landowner before accessing and collecting. Always contact local rockhounding clubs or inquiry with residents for current access conditions and landowner contact information.

faq.question: What tools do I need to collect quartz and fluorite at this New York rockhounding site?
faq.answer: Bring a small chisel or flathead screwdriver for gently extracting crystals from the dolomite host rock, a small hammer, a headlamp or flashlight for peering into cavities, and a collection bag. Work carefully along natural fracture lines to avoid breaking the delicate crystals. Sturdy gloves are recommended due to sharp crystal edges.

faq.question: What is the best season for rockhounding near Herkimer, New York?
faq.answer: May through September offers the best rockhounding conditions in upstate New York. Late spring and summer provide warm, dry weather ideal for fieldwork. Avoid late fall and winter when mud, snow, and shorter daylight hours make collecting difficult.

faq.question: Is Ace of Diamonds Mine suitable for families and beginner rockhounders?
faq.answer: Yes, this site is beginner-friendly with easy terrain, gentle slopes, and short walks from parking areas. Children ages 8 and up can participate. The combination of quartz, fluorite, and calcite provides diverse collecting opportunities. Always supervise children around loose rock and sharp crystal fragments.

faq.question: How far is Ace of Diamonds Mine from Herkimer town, and what services are nearby?
faq.answer: The mine is approximately 4 miles from the town of Herkimer, about 10 minutes by car from the Town of German Flatts. Herkimer and the nearby Mohawk area offer gas, food, water, and supplies. Consider stopping at the Herkimer Diamond Cooperative Museum to learn about the geology and history of the region before your rockhounding trip.

meta_title: Ace of Diamonds Mine Rockhounding - New York
meta_description: Find Herkimer Diamonds, quartz and fluorite at Ace of Diamonds Mine in Herkimer County, New York. Beginner-friendly collecting site with private access.
